topic.title: Standard 7: Privacy and Dignity
topic.summary: Understanding the principles of privacy and dignity, supporting individuals to maintain their privacy and dignity, and promoting individuals' rights and choices.

questions.source_criterion: 7.1a Describe what is meant by privacy and dignity
questions.prompt: What does 'dignity' mean in health and social care?

options.label: Supporting an individual's self-respect and value, and treating them in a way that makes them feel respected
Always keeping the individual in a private room
Only using formal language when speaking to the individual
Making sure the individual is never left alone

questions.explanation: Dignity means treating the individual in a way that maintains their self-respect and worth. It involves respecting their privacy, listening to them, and ensuring they feel valued as a person.

questions.source_criterion: 7.1b Understand how a care environment can support or undermine privacy and dignity
questions.prompt: Which of the following practices undermines an individual's dignity?

options.label: Discussing an individual's personal care needs within earshot of other people
Closing the curtain when providing personal care
Knocking before entering an individual's room
Addressing the individual by their preferred name

questions.explanation: Discussing personal care needs where others can hear undermines dignity. Care environments should promote privacy and dignity by ensuring personal conversations are held discreetly and care is provided respectfully.

questions.source_criterion: 7.2c Understand the importance of not disclosing information
questions.prompt: A colleague asks you about an individual you both support — what information about them can you share?

options.label: Only information they need to know to do their job, following your organisation's confidentiality policy
Any information because you both work in the same team
All the information you have so they can be fully informed
No information at all — confidentiality is absolute

questions.explanation: Information should only be shared on a 'need-to-know' basis, even between colleagues. The colleague should only have access to the information they need to provide safe care. Your organisation's confidentiality policy will guide this.

questions.source_criterion: 7.3a Support individuals to make informed choices
questions.prompt: How can you best support an individual to make an informed choice?

options.label: Provide them with the information they need in a way they can understand, and give them time to decide
Decide for them based on what you think is best
Tell them what most people in their situation choose
Only give them positive information so they make the 'right' choice

questions.explanation: To support informed choice, you must give the individual all relevant information in a format they can understand (using pictures, translation, or large print if needed), explain the risks and benefits, and give them time to decide without pressure.

questions.source_criterion: 7.3b & c Understand risk taking, risk assessment and risk enablement

options.label: Supporting individuals to understand risks and make their own choices, rather than removing all risk from their lives
Removing all possible risks to keep individuals completely safe
Ignoring risks to promote independence
A process that only applies to individuals with mental capacity issues

questions.explanation: Risk enablement means supporting individuals to understand and manage risks themselves, rather than removing their choices. It is about finding a balance between keeping someone safe and respecting their right to make their own decisions.

questions.prompt: An individual with dementia refuses to have a bath. What should you do?

options.label: Respect their refusal, document it, and try again later — unless there is an urgent health reason
Insist and get help from other staff to complete the bath
Wait until they are asleep and do it then
Bypass their consent and ask their family to decide

questions.explanation: Even if someone has dementia, they still have the right to refuse care. You should respect their refusal, document it, and try again later. Forcing care against someone's will could be a form of abuse or unlawful restraint.

questions.source_criterion: 7.4d Understand your responsibility to support individuals' rights
questions.prompt: An individual tells you they feel their rights are not being respected. What should you do?

options.label: Listen to their concerns, take them seriously, and support them to raise the issue through the proper channels
Tell them they are probably mistaken
Ignore it — they complain about everything
Decide what to do without involving the individual

questions.explanation: If an individual feels their rights are not being respected, you should listen, take their concerns seriously, and support them to raise the issue. This could involve supporting them to make a complaint, speaking to their advocate, or raising the concern yourself.

questions.source_criterion: 7.5a, b & d Understand the importance of valuing individuals
questions.prompt: What is the best way to show an individual that you value them?

options.label: Listen to them, involve them in decisions, and treat them as an individual with unique needs and preferences
Complete all care tasks as quickly as possible
Only speak to them when providing direct care
Treat all individuals the same way regardless of their preferences

questions.explanation: Valuing an individual means treating them as a unique person — listening to them, involving them in decisions, respecting their preferences, and treating them with kindness and respect. It is about recognising their worth as a person.

questions.source_criterion: 7.5c List ways to support individuals' dignity
questions.prompt: Which of the following is a way to support an individual's dignity during personal care?

options.label: Allowing them to choose what time they get up and how they want to be supported with washing and dressing
Washing and dressing them as quickly as possible without involving them
Talking to other staff members over the individual while providing care
Leaving them exposed while you prepare their clothes

questions.explanation: Supporting dignity means giving individuals choice and control over their personal care — involving them in decisions about when and how care is provided, and ensuring they are covered and comfortable throughout.

questions.prompt: Why is it important for a care worker to look after their own wellbeing?

options.label: So they can provide safe, compassionate care and be a positive role model for the individuals they support
Self-care is not important in care work
Care workers should prioritise others' needs above their own at all times
Self-care only matters outside of work hours

questions.explanation: Looking after your own physical and mental wellbeing is essential to providing good care. If you are exhausted, stressed, or unwell, you cannot give the best care to others. Taking breaks, managing stress, and seeking support when needed all contribute to better care.

questions.prompt: What does 'privacy' mean in a care setting?

options.label: Respecting an individual's personal space, and ensuring their personal information and body are not seen or discussed without their permission
Never entering an individual's room
Keeping all information secret from everyone including other care staff
Leaving the individual alone at all times

questions.explanation: Privacy means respecting an individual's personal space and boundaries. It involves ensuring their body is not unnecessarily exposed, their personal information is protected, and their private moments (like using the toilet or bathing) are not intruded upon.

questions.prompt: You overhear a colleague talking about an individual's medical history in the staff room where others can hear. What should you do?

options.label: Remind your colleague that this is a breach of confidentiality and report it if necessary
Join the conversation since everyone in the staff room works there
Ignore it — it is not your responsibility
Tell the individual's family about the conversation

questions.explanation: Discussing personal information where others can overhear is a breach of confidentiality. You have a responsibility to challenge this politely but firmly, and report it to your manager if it continues.

questions.source_criterion: 7.3a Support individuals to make informed choices
questions.prompt: An individual wants to spend their own money on something you think is unnecessary. What should you do?

options.label: Support their decision as long as they have capacity and understand the implications
Refuse to help them buy it
Tell them they are wasting their money
Ask their family to decide what is best

questions.explanation: If an individual has mental capacity and understands the implications of their decision, they have the right to spend their money as they wish — even if you think it is unwise. Your role is to support their choice, not to judge it.
